title: Enterprise Security Architecture for SAP
description: Security is a first-class architectural concern in enterprise SAP landscapes. This module covers authentication frameworks (SAML, OAuth 2.0, OIDC, X.509), authorization design patterns for PFCG and CDS DCL, secure coding to prevent SQL injection and XSS, data protection at rest and in-transit, audit logging for compliance, and penetration testing methodologies. Staff+ engineers must design security into the platform, not bolt it on after the fact.

sections.title: Authentication Framework — SAML, OAuth 2.0, OIDC & X.509

description.value:
SAP authentication architecture has evolved from the classic SAP GUI logon (single password, no MFA) to a multi-protocol Identity Federation layer that supports SAML 2.0, OAuth 2.0, OpenID Connect, X.509 client certificates, SPNego/Kerberos, and multi-factor authentication. For Staff+ engineers, the key architectural question is not which protocol to use, but how to design a trust framework that supports all protocols consistently across a heterogeneous SAP landscape.

SAML 2.0 (Security Assertion Markup Language): SAP Cloud Identity Services (IAS) acts as the Identity Provider (IdP) for BTP and S/4HANA Cloud, while on-premise SAP systems use SAP NetWeaver SAML or a third-party IdP (Azure AD, Okta, Ping). SAML assertions carry the user's identity, attributes, and authentication context. The assertion is signed by the IdP's private key and verified by the service provider (the SAP system). The critical architectural consideration is SAML trust propagation: when a BTP application calls an on-premise RFC via Cloud Connector, the SAML assertion must be forwarded — this is the SAML Bearer Assertion Flow, which requires the Cloud Connector to be configured with the IdP's metadata.

OAuth 2.0 & OpenID Connect: OAuth 2.0 is the authorization framework, OpenID Connect (OIDC) is the authentication layer built on top. BTP uses OAuth 2.0 for all service-to-service communication: the XSUAA service issues JWT tokens with scopes, audiences, and grants. The JWT token contains the user context (OIDC) and the OAuth scopes (authorization). In S/4HANA on-premise, the /sap/bc/sec/oauth2 endpoints handle token issuance. The Staff+ challenge is token lifetime management — short-lived access tokens (15-60 minutes) require refresh token rotation, which adds latency to every service call. BTP's XSUAA supports token exchange and impersonation grants for delegation scenarios.

X.509 Client Certificates: Machine-to-machine communication in SAP (RFC, Web Service, message queues) often uses X.509 client certificates rather than user-based authentication. The certificate is mapped to a SAP user via the Certificate Mapping transaction (SM30 view VUSREXTID). The certificate's Subject DN or Issuer + Serial is stored in the SAP user master and validated on every connection. For automation (CI/CD pipelines, background RFC), the certificate must have a secure private key store and automatic renewal — a common failure mode is expired certificates causing production outages.

Staff+ Perspective: A FAANG-level security architect should design a Zero Trust model for SAP: every request is authenticated and authorized regardless of network origin. This means eliminating the trusted network assumption and implementing mTLS for all inter-system communication, regardless of whether systems are in the same data center or across clouds.

* Configure SAML 2.0 on AS ABAP
* Transaction: SAML2
* Configuration steps:
* 1. Upload IdP metadata (SAML2 -> Trusted Providers -> Import)
* 2. Configure Local Provider (SAML2 -> Local Provider)
*    - Signing key: use TLS/SSL certificate or dedicated SAML key
*    - Default logon method: SAML 2.0
* 3. Map IdP attributes to SAP user fields
*    - E-Mail -> SMTP_ADDR for user auto-provisioning
*    - UserPrincipalName -> BNAME
* 4. Enable SAML Bearer Assertion for OAuth 2.0
*    - /sap/bc/sec/oauth2/support -> enable Bearer Assertion

" OAuth 2.0 Client Registration
" /sap/bc/sec/oauth2/admin
" Scope definitions for resource APIs
" Client credentials grant for service-to-service
" Authorization code grant for interactive Fiori
" Note: OAuth 2.0 requires SSL (TLS 1.2+) to protect tokens

" ABAP code: JWT validation in custom service
DATA lv_jwt TYPE string.
DATA lo_oauth2_validator TYPE REF TO cl_oauth2_s_token_validator.

lo_oauth2_validator = cl_oauth2_s_token_validator=>create(
  iv_scope      = 'Z_MY_SCOPE'
  iv_token_type = 'Bearer'
  iv_header     = lv_jwt ).

try.
    lo_oauth2_validator->if_oauth2_token_validator~validate( ).
    DATA(lv_user) = lo_oauth2_validator->if_oauth2_token_validator~get_user( ).
    DATA(lv_tenant) = lo_oauth2_validator->if_oauth2_token_validator~get_tenant( ).
  catch cx_oauth2_token_invalid INTO DATA(lx_err).
    " Handle invalid/expired token
endtry.

* Staff+ Pattern: Use X.509 certificate for RFC automation
* Certificate must be mapped in SM30 (VUSREXTID)
* Set PSE (Personal Security Environment) via STRUST
* Use RFC with gx- option: CALL FUNCTION ... DESTINATION 'MY_QOUT'
*   DESTINATION must have SSL enabled and certificate validation

sections.codeBlock:
" OAuth 2.0 JWT validation in custom ABAP service
DATA lo_oauth2_validator TYPE REF TO cl_oauth2_s_token_validator.
lo_oauth2_validator = cl_oauth2_s_token_validator=>create(
  iv_scope      = 'Z_MY_SCOPE'
  iv_token_type = 'Bearer'
  iv_header     = lv_jwt ).
TRY.
    lo_oauth2_validator->if_oauth2_token_validator~validate( ).
    DATA(lv_user) = lo_oauth2_validator->if_oauth2_token_validator~get_user( ).
    DATA(lv_tenant) = lo_oauth2_validator->if_oauth2_token_validator~get_tenant( ).
  CATCH cx_oauth2_token_invalid INTO DATA(lx_err).
    " Handle invalid/expired token
ENDTRY.

" SAML 2.0 config steps:
" 1. SM30 VUSREXTID — map X.509 cert to user
" 2. STRUST — create PSE for SSL server
" 3. SAML2 — configure IdP metadata and local provider
" 4. Enable SAML Bearer Assertion for OAuth 2.0:
"    /sap/bc/sec/oauth2/support -> enable Bearer Assertion

decisionTree.title: Choosing the Right Authentication Protocol for SAP Integration
decisionTree.prompt: Should I use SAML 2.0, OAuth 2.0 with OIDC, or X.509 client certificates for this SAP integration?
decisionTree.firstQuestion: "Is this a human user interacting through a browser, or a machine-to-machine service call?" If human + browser, use SAML 2.0 (for SAP Fiori/Portal) or OAuth 2.0 with OIDC (for BTP apps). If machine-to-machine, use OAuth 2.0 Client Credentials or X.509 certificates for RFC calls.

branches.condition: Human user, browser-based (Fiori, Portal, BTP cockpit)
branches.path: SAML 2.0 with IAS/IdP. User authenticates via IdP, SAML assertion forwarded to SAP system. Best for: Fiori launchpad, Enterprise Portal, BTP subscriptions. Supports MFA, conditional access.

branches.condition: Machine-to-machine, BTP service calling another BTP service
branches.path: OAuth 2.0 Client Credentials with XSUAA. No user context — service account. JWT token with scopes. Best for: service-to-service API calls, event-driven integrations.

branches.condition: Machine-to-machine, on-premise RFC with no user interaction
branches.path: X.509 client certificate via STRUST PSE. Certificate mapped to user in SM30 VUSREXTID. Best for: background RFC, batch jobs, ALE/IDoc processing. No password rotation needed.

decisionTree.staffPlusTip: At Staff+, never propose a single protocol for the entire landscape. The right answer is a protocol matrix: 'SAML for human users via Fiori, OAuth Client Credentials for BTP service-to-service, X.509 for on-premise RFC, and a token exchange flow for the BTP-to-on-premise bridge.' Show you understand the holistic trust architecture.

sections.title: Authorization & Role Design — PFCG, SU24 & Least Privilege

description.value:
SAP authorization design follows the Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) model, implemented through PFCG (Profile Generator). The authorization objects (auth objects) are the atomic units — they represent a protected action on a business object with specific field values. A role aggregates multiple authorization objects with their field values. Complex roles are composed into composite roles, and users are assigned to roles via user master records (SU01).

Authorization Object Architecture: Each auth object has up to 10 authorization fields. For example, F_BKPF_BUK (Financial Accounting: Document: Authorization for Company Codes) has fields: ACTVT (activity, e.g., 01=Create, 02=Change, 03=Display, 06=Delete) and BUKRS (company code). The values can be specific (1000), range (1000–2000), wildcard (* for all), or a table of values. The authorization check in ABAP: AUTHORITY-CHECK OBJECT 'F_BKPF_BUK' ID 'ACTVT' FIELD '03' ID 'BUKRS' FIELD '1000'. The runtime evaluates the user's profile against the requested values and returns sy-subrc = 0 for authorized, 4 for unauthorized.

SU24 — Authorization Defaults: Transaction SU24 maintains the suggested authorization objects for each transaction code. When a user calls a transaction, the system checks the auth objects defined in SU24. If the user lacks authorization for any object, the transaction is blocked. The Staff+ challenge is managing SU24: too many objects per transaction creates authorization complexity, too few creates security gaps. The pattern is to define critical auth objects at the transaction level and sensitive field values at the organization level (e.g., company code via authorization field derived from the user's org assignment).

Least Privilege Pattern: The principle of least privilege dictates that users should have the minimum authorization necessary to perform their job. In practice, this means: (1) Avoid wildcards for sensitive fields (company code, plant, sales org). (2) Use derived roles (from organizational assignments) rather than manually maintained roles. (3) Implement Firefighter / Emergency Access management (transaction SUGR) for break-glass scenarios. (4) Automate role provisioning via IDM (Identity Management) to eliminate manual SU01 changes. FAANG engineers apply the same principle in cloud IAM — never grant more permissions than needed, and use attribute-based access control (ABAC) where possible.

y2="350" class="sys-edge"/><text class="hi-lbl" x="350" y="365">AUDIT: SUIM reports / SECATT checks / Role review cycle</text></svg>

" Classic ABAP authorization check
AUTHORITY-CHECK OBJECT 'F_BKPF_BUK'
  ID 'ACTVT' FIELD '03'
  ID 'BUKRS' FIELD '1000'.

IF sy-subrc <> 0.
  MESSAGE e000(z_auth) WITH 'Display company code 1000'.
ENDIF.

" OK code (synced):
AUTHORITY-CHECK OBJECT 'M_MATE_STA'
  ID 'ACTVT' FIELD '02'
  ID 'MATNR' DUMMY
  ID 'WERKS' FIELD 'PL01'
  ID 'LGORT' DUMMY
  ID 'BWLVS' DUMMY.
" DUMMY skips the field check (used when field value doesn't matter)

" SU24 best practice: define minimum required auth objects per tcode
" Transaction SU24 -> Authorization Defaults for Transactions
" For custom transaction Z_MY_APP:
"   Add object Z_AUTH_ITEM with fields:
"     ACTVT = 01,02,03  (Create, Change, Display)
"     Z_APPID = 'MY_APP'
"     Z_ORGID = *  (checked dynamically in code)

" Staff+ Pattern: Dynamic authorization using BAdI / enhancement
" BAdI: BBP_AUTHORITY_CHECK for SRM
" Enhancement: SUSR_BADIP_AUTH_CHECK for custom field derivations
" FAANG equivalent: ABAC (Attribute-Based Access Control)
"   Auth decision = f(User.Attributes, Resource.Attributes, Environment)
"   Example: Allow display if User.Region = Resource.Region AND time < 6 PM
"   Implement using AUTH_CHECK_OBJECT with computed field values

sections.codeBlock:
" Classic ABAP authorization check
AUTHORITY-CHECK OBJECT 'F_BKPF_BUK'
  ID 'ACTVT' FIELD '03'
  ID 'BUKRS' FIELD '1000'.
IF sy-subrc <> 0.
  MESSAGE e000(z_auth) WITH 'Display company code 1000'.
ENDIF.

" OK code with DUMMY (skip field):
AUTHORITY-CHECK OBJECT 'M_MATE_STA'
  ID 'ACTVT' FIELD '02'
  ID 'MATNR' DUMMY
  ID 'WERKS' FIELD 'PL01'
  ID 'LGORT' DUMMY
  ID 'BWLVS' DUMMY.

" SU24: set minimum auth objects per transaction
" Transaction SU24 -> Authorization Defaults for Transactions
" For Z_MY_APP: add Z_AUTH_ITEM with ACTVT=01,02,03
"
" Staff+ pattern: dynamic auth (ABAC)
" Auth decision = f(User.Role, Resource.Region, Time)
" Implement using BAdI SUSR_BADIP_AUTH_CHECK

organizationalDimension.title: Migrating from Legacy RBAC to Attribute-Based Access Control
organizationalDimension.migrationStrategy: The 'Layered Authorization' migration: Phase 1 (months 1-3) adds ABAC attributes (user region, resource sensitivity, time of day) as authorization dimensions on top of existing PFCG roles — PFCG handles 'what you can do' while ABAC adds 'where and when you can do it.' Phase 2 (months 4-8) migrates high-risk auth objects (financial, sensitive data) to ABAC-only checks, with PFCG as fallback. Phase 3 (months 9-12) sunsets the lowest 20% of PFCG auth objects that are fully covered by ABAC.

stakeholders.concern: ABAC introduces dynamic authorization that is harder to audit than static PFCG roles
stakeholders.address: Log every ABAC decision with the attribute values that produced it. Generate an 'effective permissions' report per user that shows what ABAC allows vs what PFCG allows. Audit becomes comparing two permission sets, not one.

stakeholders.concern: ABAC requires new infrastructure (attribute directory, policy engine) that we do not have
stakeholders.address: Start with BAdI SUSR_BADIP_AUTH_CHECK — no new infrastructure. Attribute directory is a simple Z table initially. Policy engine is ABAP code. Only move to dedicated infrastructure (Ping, Axiomatics) when RBAC-to-ABAC migration is >30% complete.

stakeholders.concern: Dynamic authorization may block users at unexpected times
stakeholders.address: If ABAC denies access, display the specific attribute that caused the denial ('Access denied: your region is EU but this document is in region APAC'). User can request temporary override with approval workflow.

organizationalDimension.adoptionPhases: Phase 1: ABAC attributes on top of PFCG. Phase 2: Migrate high-risk objects. Phase 3: Sunset redundant PFCG objects. Phase 4 (12+ months): Full ABAC with attribute-based provisioning.
organizationalDimension.metrics: Month 3: 3 attributes deployed (region, sensitivity, time). Month 6: 20% of auth checks use ABAC. Month 9: 50% of auth checks use ABAC. Month 12: 80% auth coverage, 20% PFCG reduction.
organizationalDimension.antiPatterns: (1) ABAC becomes as complex as RBAC with too many attributes — keep to 5-7 core attributes. (2) ABAC bypass for performance — 'this transaction is too slow with ABAC, let us add a PFCG bypass' — defeats the purpose. (3) No ABAC audit trail — regulators need to know WHY access was granted, not just WHO.

sections.title: Secure Coding — SQL Injection, XSS & Code Scanning

description.value:
ABAP applications face the same web security threats as any enterprise platform — SQL injection, cross-site scripting (XSS), cross-site request forgery (CSRF), command injection, and path traversal — but the attack surface is unique because of the dual ABAP/HANA runtime, BSP (Business Server Pages) for Web Dynpro, and the wide range of communication protocols (RFC, HTTP, OData, ALE/IDoc). Staff+ engineers must ensure code is secure by construction, not relying on post-deployment scans alone.

SQL Injection in Open SQL vs AMDP: Classic Open SQL using SELECT (lv_fields) FROM (lv_table) with dynamic table names or field lists is the most common injection vector in ABAP. SAP has introduced the @ escape syntax (e.g., SELECT * FROM @(lv_table_name)) to make dynamic SQL safer, but the best defense is to avoid dynamic SQL altogether. In AMDP, SQLScript uses SQL injection in the same way as any SQL dialect — concatenating input into EXECUTE IMMEDIATE or dynamic WHERE clauses opens the door. The mitigation is always use parameterized queries (WHERE MATNR = :lv_material) and validate all input that cannot be parameterized against an allowlist. The CL_ABAP_DYN_PRG class provides check_whitelist_str and check_whitelist_tab methods for validating dynamic content.

XSS in BSP & Web Dynpro: BSP applications include HTML tags and JavaScript-like code in their page structure. User input rendered without escaping creates stored or reflected XSS vulnerabilities. The cl_http_server built-in encoding functions (escape_html, escape_url, escape_javascript) must be applied to every user-controllable value before rendering. For Web Dynpro (both ABAP and Floorplan Manager), the framework automatically escapes output, but custom HTML in CL_WD_HTML_TOOLTIP or CL_WD_LAUNCHPAD bypasses this protection. GateRunner (SAP's web application firewall) adds a perimeter defense but should not replace proper output encoding at the application layer.

Code Scanning & SAST: SAP's Code Vulnerability Analyzer (transcation CVA) performs static analysis for security vulnerabilities. CVA checks for: SQL injection (dynamic SQL without whitelist), XSS (unencoded output in BSP), path traversal (file operations with user input), command injection (SYSTEM/CALL statements with concatenated strings), hardcoded credentials (passwords in source code), and insecure cryptographic storage (weak encryption algorithms). CVA integrates with ABAP Test Cockpit (ATC) for continuous scanning in the development pipeline — every transport request should trigger CVA checks before moving to quality. FAANG teams use similar SAST tools (Semgrep, CodeQL, SonarQube) with CI/CD gating — no vulnerable code reaches production.

" INSECURE: Dynamic Open SQL — SQL injection possible
DATA(lv_where) = |MATNR = '| && lv_input && |'|.
SELECT * FROM mara INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_mara) WHERE (lv_where).
" Attacker input: ' OR 1=1 OR MATNR = ' -> returns all rows

" SECURE: Parameterized Open SQL
SELECT * FROM mara INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_mara_safe)
  WHERE matnr = @lv_input.

" INSECURE: Dynamic table name
SELECT * FROM (lv_tabname) INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_dyn).

" SECURE: Whitelist validation
cl_abap_dyn_prg=>check_whitelist_str(
  EXPORTING
    val      = lv_tabname
    whitelist = VALUE #( ( 'MARA' ) ( 'MARC' ) ( 'MARD' ) )
  EXCEPTIONS
    not_allowed = 4 ).
IF sy-subrc = 0.
  SELECT * FROM (lv_tabname) INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_dyn_safe).
ENDIF.

" AMDP secure: parameterized SQLScript
" BAD:
" sql = 'SELECT * FROM MARA WHERE ' || :iv_filter;
" EXECUTE IMMEDIATE :sql;
"
" GOOD: parameterized
" SELECT * INTO :lt_result FROM MARA
"   WHERE MATNR = :iv_matnr;

" XSS prevention in BSP
" BAD: <%= lv_user_input %>
" GOOD: <%= cl_http_utility=>escape_html( lv_user_input ) %>

" SAP Code Vulnerability Analyzer (CVA) configuration:
" /nCVA -> Create check variant
"   - Include all check categories:
"     SQL injection, XSS, Command Injection, Path Traversal,
"     Hardcoded Credentials, Weak Cryptography
"   - Severity threshold: Error (fails ATC)
"   - Run on: ALL objects in transport request
" /nSCI -> Code Inspector (for custom ABAP checks)
" Use ATC variant Z_ATC_FULL with both CI + CVA checks

CSRF Protection: SAP Fiori and OData services use CSRF tokens to prevent cross-site request forgery. The client must fetch a token via a GET request to the service (with custom header X-CSRF-Token: Fetch) and include it in subsequent write requests. For custom HTTP handlers (e.g., IF_HTTP_EXTENSION), the framework does not enforce CSRF automatically — the developer must implement token validation using CL_ABAP_CSRF_TOKEN. In Web Dynpro, the framework auto-generates CSRF tokens for all form submissions, but custom SAPEVENT URLs may bypass protection. The Staff+ pattern is to implement a central CSRF filter (BAdI enhancement or HTTP filter) that checks all state-changing requests — analogous to Spring Security's CsrfFilter in Java.

Cryptography Standards: ABAP supports cryptographic operations via the SSF (Secure Store & Forward) framework, the SSF API (function modules SSF_KRN_ENVELOPE, SSF_KRN_DEVELOPE), and the ABAP Crypto library (CL_SEC_SXML_WRITER for XML digital signatures). SAP's default encryption for stored data is AES-256 with the PSE (Personal Security Environment) key store. For hashing, use SHA-256 or SHA-3 (not MD5 or SHA-1, which are deprecated). Passwords must use bcrypt or PBKDF2 with a configurable work factor — SAP standard uses SHA1( password + salt ) which is outdated; custom implementations should use CL_ABAP_PASSWORD_HASH for stronger algorithms. Never implement custom cryptographic algorithms — use SAP's certified cryptographic library (CommonCryptoLib).

sections.codeBlock:
" INSECURE: Dynamic SQL — injection possible
DATA(lv_where) = |MATNR = '| && lv_input && |'|.
SELECT * FROM mara INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_mara) WHERE (lv_where).
" Attacker: ' OR 1=1 OR MATNR = ' -> returns all rows
"
" SECURE: Parameterized
SELECT * FROM mara INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_mara_safe)
  WHERE matnr = @lv_input.
"
" Dynamic table name with whitelist:
cl_abap_dyn_prg=>check_whitelist_str(
  EXPORTING val = lv_tabname
    whitelist = VALUE #( ( 'MARA' ) ( 'MARC' ) ( 'MARD' ) )
  EXCEPTIONS not_allowed = 4 ).
IF sy-subrc = 0.
  SELECT * FROM (lv_tabname) INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_dyn).
ENDIF.
"
" XSS prevention in BSP:
" BAD: <%= lv_user_input %>
" GOOD: <%= cl_http_utility=>escape_html( lv_user_input ) %>
"
" Use ATC variant with CVA + CI checks
" /nCVA -> Create check variant, include all SQL/XSS checks

adversarialResponse.title: Handling 'Your secure coding requirements slow development velocity by 30%. Engineers will bypass them.'
adversarialResponse.challenge: "You want every SELECT to use @-parameters, every BSP output escaped, and every transport to pass ATC with zero errors. That will slow development by 30% and engineers will find ways around it."
adversarialResponse.badResponse: "Security is non-negotiable. Engineers must follow the process." (no empathy, ignores the real friction)
adversarialResponse.goodResponse: "You are describing a legitimate tension between security and velocity. Let me address it with a tiered approach. Low-risk code (reports, utility classes) uses automated scanning with auto-fix — ATC suggests the fix and applies it. Medium-risk code (OData services, BSP applications) requires human review but we provide IDE plugins that flag issues before commit, so the review is a rubber stamp 80% of the time. High-risk code (RFC modules, payment processing, PII handling) requires manual review with a 24-hour SLA. The key metric: the automation should catch 90% of SQL injection issues before human review. We invest in tooling that makes secure coding the path of least resistance — not more gates. For example, our ABAP Git plugin auto-converts dynamic SELECT to parameterized SELECT when possible. Engineers do not bypass security when security is automated into the toolchain."
adversarialResponse.pattern: empathy-with-tiered-defense — acknowledge the velocity concern, design a risk-tiered approach that automates 90% of enforcement, invest in tooling that makes secure coding the easy path. The Staff+ signal is understanding that security must be designed into developer workflow, not imposed as a gate.

sections.title: Data Security at Rest & In-Transit

description.value:
Data security spans two dimensions: data at rest (database encryption, backup encryption, HANA data volume encryption) and data in-transit (network encryption, TLS configuration, RFC trust). In an SAP landscape handling financial, personal, and trade-sensitive data, a breach in either dimension can cause regulatory penalties, reputational damage, and operational disruption. Staff+ engineers design the data security architecture holistically — not as separate controls but as a layered defense that covers all data lifecycle stages.

Data at Rest — HANA Encryption: SAP HANA offers multiple layers of encryption. (1) HANA Data Volume Encryption: AES-256 encryption of the data volumes on disk (persistent data). This is transparent to the database engine — all reads and writes go through encryption/decryption with <3% performance overhead on modern hardware. The encryption keys are stored in HANA's secure key store, separate from the data files. (2) HANA Backup Encryption: Backups are encrypted using the same key infrastructure or a separate backup key. Critical for offsite backup scenarios — tape or cloud storage must maintain encryption. (3) Column-Level Encryption: For highly sensitive fields (SSN, credit card, bank account), SAP provides the SECSTORE mechanism or custom encryption via ABAP classes. Note that column-level encryption prevents HANA from using indexes on those columns — queries that filter on encrypted fields must decrypt each row, causing full table scans. (4) Application-Level Encryption: Data encrypted at the ABAP layer before writing to the database. This is the strongest protection — even a database administrator cannot read the data. But it prevents database-side processing (no SUM, GROUP BY, JOIN on encrypted fields).

Data in-Transit — TLS & RFC: All SAP communication should use TLS 1.2 or higher. SAP's STUST transaction manages the SSL server certificates (PSE files), and STRUST manages the SSL client. For RFC communication, SAP supports Secure Network Communications (SNC) using Kerberos or X.509. The SNC library (gssapi32.dll on Windows, libgssap.so on Linux) provides authentication and encryption for RFC dialogs. For HTTP/S connections (OData, SOAP, ICM), the Internet Communication Manager (ICM) handles TLS termination. The minimum TLS version is configured in the ICM profile parameter: icm/SSL/TLS_VERSION_MIN = 1.2. For internal system-to-system communication within the same secure zone, encrypted RFC still provides defense-in-depth — a compromised internal network should not expose plaintext SAP traffic.

Key Management: All encryption keys must be managed through a Hardware Security Module (HSM) or a secure key management service (KMS). SAP supports PKCS#11 interfaces for HSM integration. Keys must be rotated regularly — HANA data volume keys can be rotated via ALTER SYSTEM ROTATE DATA VOLUME KEY without downtime. Backup keys should be rotated after every full backup cycle. The worst pattern is hardcoded encryption keys in ABAP source code — this is a critical CVA finding and a SOC 2 audit failure.

/ Zero-downtime / Audit trail</text><rect class="sys-box" x="475" y="335" width="190" height="35"/><text class="sys-lbl" x="570" y="352">Compliance Validation</text><text class="sys-lbl" x="570" " HANA key rotation (system privilege: DATA VOLUME ADMIN)
" ALTER SYSTEM ROTATE DATA VOLUME KEY;
" This rotates the key without database restart.

" HANA backup encryption
" CREATE BACKUP DATA USING FILE ('/backup/sap_backup')
"   WITH ENCRYPTION ('AES256', 'key_name');
" 
" Monitor backup encryption:
" SELECT * FROM SYS.M_BACKUP_CATALOG WHERE ENCRYPTED = 'TRUE';

" ABAP: SECSTORE for field-level encryption
DATA lv_plaintext TYPE string VALUE '4111111111111111'.
DATA lv_encrypted TYPE xstring.

CALL FUNCTION 'SECSTORE_ENCRYPT'
  EXPORTING
    iv_content = cl_abap_codepage=>convert_to( lv_plaintext )
    iv_id      = 'Z_CC_MASK'
  IMPORTING
    ev_encrypted = lv_encrypted.
" Store lv_encrypted in DB (type RAWSTRING / LRAW)
" Use SECSTORE_DECRYPT to retrieve

" SNC configuration for RFC encryption
" Profile parameters:
" snc/identity/as = p:CN=SAP_System_SNC, O=MyCompany, C=DE
" snc/gssapi_lib = /usr/sap/lib/libgssapkrb5.so
" snc/accept_insecure_cpic = 0
" snc/accept_insecure_gui = 0
" snc/accept_insecure_rfc = 0
" snc/permit_insecure_rfc = 0  (reject unencrypted RFC)
" snc/data_protection = 3 (max: encryption + integrity)
" Use SM30 V_SNCONF to verify SNC trust relationships

" TLS minimum version in ICM
" Profile: icm/SSL/TLS_VERSION_MIN = 1.2
" Check with: disp+work -param icm/SSL/TLS_VERSION_MIN
" For client connections, also set:
" icm/SSL/TLS_VERSION_MAX = 1.3 (enable if supported)

sections.codeBlock:
" HANA key rotation:
" ALTER SYSTEM ROTATE DATA VOLUME KEY;
"
" HANA backup encryption:
" CREATE BACKUP DATA USING FILE ('/backup/sap_backup')
"   WITH ENCRYPTION ('AES256', 'key_name');
"
" ABAP: SECSTORE for field-level encryption
DATA lv_plaintext TYPE string VALUE '4111111111111111'.
DATA lv_encrypted TYPE xstring.
CALL FUNCTION 'SECSTORE_ENCRYPT'
  EXPORTING
    iv_content = cl_abap_codepage=>convert_to( lv_plaintext )
    iv_id      = 'Z_CC_MASK'
  IMPORTING
    ev_encrypted = lv_encrypted.
" Store lv_encrypted in DB (type RAWSTRING)
"
" SNC config for RFC encryption:
" snc/data_protection = 3  (encryption + integrity)
" snc/accept_insecure_rfc = 0
" snc/gssapi_lib = /usr/sap/lib/libgssapkrb5.so
"
" TLS minimum: icm/SSL/TLS_VERSION_MIN = 1.2

sections.title: Audit Logging & Compliance — SOC 2, GDPR, SOX

description.value:
Audit logging is a regulatory requirement — not optional. For SAP systems subject to SOC 2 (Service Organization Controls), GDPR (General Data Protection Regulation), SOX (Sarbanes-Oxley), or PCI DSS (Payment Card Industry Data Security Standard), the audit log must provide a tamper-proof record of who did what, when, and from where. SAP's audit logging framework is built on the Security Audit Log (transaction SM19/SM20) and the ABAP Audit Logging API (IF_ABAP_AUDIT_LOG).

Security Audit Log (SM19/SM20): The Security Audit Log records security-relevant events at the system level: successful and failed logons, RFC connections, transaction starts, authorization failures, and profile changes. The configuration is in SM19: you define which events to audit, the log file size, and the retention policy. The log is stored in file system files (audit_<YYYYMMDD>_<HOST>.aud) and can be exported for SIEM integration. The critical configuration is log mode: write to file only (fastest but no real-time monitoring), write to database table SNCSYSLOG (for real-time query via SM20), or write to both. For compliance, the database write mode is recommended — it enables real-time alerting and simplifies log review.

ABAP Audit Logging API (IF_ABAP_AUDIT_LOG): For application-level auditing, SAP provides the IF_ABAP_AUDIT_LOG interface. This API allows custom code to write audit log entries. The key method is log_entity with parameters for the audit category, message, user, client, and additional data. The audit log entries are written to the application log (SLG1) or to a dedicated audit database table. The Staff+ consideration is performance: each audit log write is a database commit. If your application processes 1000 records per second and you audit each record individually, you create 1000 commits per second — each commit is an I/O operation. The pattern is to buffer audit entries in an internal table and flush them periodically (e.g., every 100 records or every 5 seconds), controlled by a batch job that writes to the audit table in bulk.

GDPR Compliance — Data Masking & Retention: GDPR requires that personal data can be anonymized, restricted, or deleted on request. SAP provides Information Lifecycle Management (ILM) with the ILM_STORE and ILM_DESTRUCTION transactions. For ABAP applications, personal data is identified using the Personal Data definition in transaction SPDD (or using SAP's Data Protection API CL_DATAPROTECTION_API). All audit logs containing personal data must be retained according to the retention policy (typically 1-3 years for access logs, 6-10 years for financial records) and then securely deleted. For SOC 2, user access reviews (UAR) must be conducted quarterly — SUIM reports generate the user-to-role-to-auth-object matrix for review. FAANG teams automate UAR using identity governance tools (e.g., Saviynt, SailPoint) that integrate with SAP via SCIM or custom RFC interfaces.

xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><style>.hi-box{fill:var(--bg-secondary);stroke:var(--text-primary);stroke-width:2;rx:8}.hi-edge{fill:var(--bg-secondary);stroke:var(--brand-primary);stroke-width:2;rx:6}.hi-lbl{fill:var(--text-primary);font-family:system-ui;font-size:11px;font-weight:600;text-anchor:middle}.sys-box{fill:var(--bg-secondary);stroke:var(--border-default);stroke-width:1.5;rx:6}.sys-lbl{fill:var(--text-primary);font-family:system-ui;font-size:9px;text-anchor:middle}.sys-edge{stroke:var(--brand-primary);stroke-width:2;fill:none;marker-end:url(#arrow)}.flow-pulse{animation:pulse (IF_ABAP_AUDIT_LOG)</text><text class="sys-lbl" x="515" y="103">CRUD operations / Business events / Masking</text><line x1="185" y1="110" x2="185" y2="140" class="sys-edge"/><line x1="515" y1="110" x2="515" y2="140" class="sys-edge"/><rect class="sys-box" x="40" y="140" width="190" height="35"/><text class="sys-lbl" x="135" y="155">Log File (.aud)</text><text class="sys-lbl" x="135" y="168">Rollover / Retention</text><rect class="sys-box" x="255" y="140" width="190" height="35"/><text class="sys-lbl" x="350" y="155">DB Table (SNCSYSLOG)</text><text class="sys-lbl" x="350" y="168">Real-time query / SIEM</text><rect class="sys-box" x="470" y="140" width="190" height="35"/><text class="sys-lbl" x="565" y="155">Application Log (SLG1)</text><text class="sys-lbl" x="565" y="168">Buffered write / Bulk flush</text><line x1="135" y1="175" x2="135" y2="200" class="sys-edge"/><line x1="350" y1="175" x2="350" y2="200" class="sys-edge"/><line x1="565" y1="175" x2="565" y2="200" class="sys-edge"/><rect class="hi-edge" x="100" y="200" width="500" height="35"/><text class="hi-lbl" x="350" y="220">SIEM Integration — Syslog / Elasticsearch / Splunk / QRadar</text><text class="sys-lbl" x="350" y="232">CEF format / RFC 5424 / Real-time alerts</text><line x1="200" y1="235" x2="200" y2="265" class="sys-edge"/><line x1="500" y1="235" x2="500" y2="265" class="sys-edge"/><rect class="sys-box" x="40" y="265" width="195" height="45"/><text class="sys-lbl" x="137" y="285">SOC 2: UAR quarterly</text><text class="sys-lbl" x="137" y="300">SUIM / Governance tools</text><rect class="sys-box" x="252" y="265" width="195" height="45"/><text class="sys-lbl" x="350" y="285">GDPR: Data masking + deletion</text><text class="sys-lbl" x="350" y="300">ILM / CL_DATAPROTECTION_API</text><rect class="sys-box" x="465" y="265" width="195" height="45"/><text class="sys-lbl" x="562" y="285">SOX: Segregation of duties</text><text class="sys-lbl" x="562" y="300">SoD matrix / Role conflict detection</text><line x1="137" y1="310" x2="137" y2="340" class="sys-edge"/><line x1="350" y1="310" x2="350" y2="340" class="sys-edge"/><line x1="562" y1="310" x2="562" y2="340" class="sys-edge"/><rect class="hi-edge" x="150" y="340" width="400" height="35"/><text class="hi-lbl" x="350" y="362">Continuous Compliance — Automated Checks + Quarterly Reviews</text></svg>

" Application audit logging — IF_ABAP_AUDIT_LOG
DATA lo_audit_log TYPE REF TO if_abap_audit_log.
data lv_audit_data TYPE string.

lo_audit_log = cl_abap_audit_log=>create( ).

lv_audit_data = |User { sy-uname } modified material { lv_matnr }|
  && |from status { lv_old_status } to { lv_new_status }|.

lo_audit_log->log_entity(
  EXPORTING
    iv_entity    = 'Z_MATERIAL'
    iv_entity_id = lv_matnr
    iv_operation = 'UPDATE'
    iv_category  = 'C' " Change
    iv_message   = lv_audit_data
    iv_user      = sy-uname
    iv_client    = sy-mandt
    iv_program   = sy-cprog ).
" This creates an entry in the security audit log (SM20).
" For compliance, always audit: CREATE, UPDATE, DELETE, and
"   authorization failures.
" Do NOT audit: DISPLAY (too noisy, high volume).

" Buffered audit pattern for high-volume scenarios
" Define a buffer table in class:
" CLASS zcl_audit_buffer DEFINITION.
"   PUBLIC SECTION.
"     METHODS add_entry IMPORTING is_entry TYPE zaudit_entry.
"     METHODS flush.
"   PRIVATE SECTION.
"     DATA mt_buffer TYPE STANDARD TABLE OF zaudit_entry.
"     CONSTANTS mc_batch_size TYPE i VALUE 100.
" ENDCLASS.
"
" Call ADD_ENTRY in business logic; call FLUSH every 5 seconds
" or at program end. FLUSH writes all buffered entries in a
" single RFC call or ABAP database commit.

" SOX Segregation of Duties: Use transaction SUIM O:
" Report: RSEC_SOD_CHECK
" Check: Can a user both create a PO and approve it?
"   Conflict: M_MEST_BSA (Create PO) + M_MEST_FRG (Release PO)
"   Resolution: Different auth objects -> different roles
"               Role review -> quarterly UAR

" GDPR: Data retention in ILM
" /nILM_STORE -> Define audit log retention (Storage Policy)
" /nILM_DESTRUCTION -> Schedule destruction of expired logs
" Monitor: IRMPOL / ILM_AREA / ILM_OBJECT

sections.codeBlock:
" Application audit logging — IF_ABAP_AUDIT_LOG
DATA lo_audit_log TYPE REF TO if_abap_audit_log.
lo_audit_log = cl_abap_audit_log=>create( ).
DATA(lv_msg) = |User { sy-uname } modified material { lv_matnr }|
  && |from { lv_old_status } to { lv_new_status }|.
lo_audit_log->log_entity(
  iv_entity    = 'Z_MATERIAL'
  iv_entity_id = lv_matnr
  iv_operation = 'UPDATE'
  iv_category  = 'C'
  iv_message   = lv_msg
  iv_user      = sy-uname
  iv_client    = sy-mandt
  iv_program   = sy-cprog ).
" Buffered audit for high volume:
" Buffer 100 entries, flush every 5 seconds
" One DB commit per flush instead of per entry
"
" GDPR: data retention in ILM
" /nILM_STORE -> define storage policy
" /nILM_DESTRUCTION -> schedule destruction

sections.title: Penetration Testing & Vulnerability Management

description.value:
Penetration testing for SAP systems requires a specialized approach — standard web application pentesting (OWASP Top 10) must be augmented with SAP-specific attack vectors: RFC function module injection, ABAP in HTTP header injection, BSP/Web Dynpro specific XSS, system command injection through RFC, ABAP Gateway buffer overflow, and configuration weaknesses (SAP* with default password, RFC trusted systems without SNC). A Staff+ engineer understands both the offensive and defensive perspectives — you cannot design effective defenses without knowing how an attacker thinks.

OWASP for SAP — The SAP Security Hardening Checklist: (1) Authentication: Test default passwords (SAP*/PASS, DDIC/19920706, TMSADM/PASSWORD). Verify MFA enforcement for all administrative access. (2) RFC Hardening: Verify that RFC gateways restrict access by IP — gw/reg_info and gw/acl_mode parameters. Test that SNC is enforced on all RFC connections. (3) ICM Hardening: Test for HTTP header injection (e.g., X-Forwarded-For spoofing, Host header manipulation). Verify that unnecessary ICM services (HTTP, SMTP, HTTPS) are disabled. (4) Message Server: Verify that the message server is not exposed to the internet. Test for information disclosure via ms/port. (5) ABAP Gateway: Test for buffer overflow vulnerabilities by sending oversized payloads. (6) User Management: Verify that user types (Dialog, Service, System, Communication, Reference) are correctly assigned. Service and System users should have password-locked or non-dialog logon. (7) Transport Management: Verify that transport users (TMSADM, DDIC) are not used for daily operations. Test transport (STR) configuration for unauthorized access.

Security Notes & Patch Management: SAP releases monthly Security Patch Day (second Tuesday) patches that address CVEs. Each patch is referenced by an SAP Security Note number. The SAP Security Patch Manager (transaction SPAU, SPAM, or the newer SAP Maintenance Planner) applies these patches. The critical decision is the patch window — often SAP systems have monthly patch windows that conflict with business cycles. Staff+ engineers design a patch strategy that prioritizes critical CVEs (CVSS >= 9.0) within 48 hours, high severity (7.0-8.9) within 2 weeks, and medium/low within 30 days. This is the same risk-based patching methodology used by FAANG companies: the vulnerability severity drives the patch velocity, not the calendar.

Automated Vulnerability Scanning: Beyond manual pentesting, automated scans (e.g., Onapsis, SAP Security Scan — transaction /n/SSA/PROFILE, Cloud Security Alliance STAR) run continuously. These tools check for: missing security notes (compare against SAP's CVA database), insecure configuration (RFC ACL, SNC, ICM, TLS), default credentials, authorization risks (SoD conflicts, critical auth objects with wildcards), and custom code vulnerabilities. The scan results should feed the team's backlog with risk-ranked remediation. At FAANG scale, every production change is gated by a security review that includes SAST results.

* Profile parameter security check
* Critical parameters:
*   gw/reg_info        = /etc/sap/reg_info (ACL file, not empty)
*   gw/acl_mode        = 1 (deny anonymous registration)
*   login/min_password_lng = 8
*   login/password_change_waittime = 1 (no immediate reuse)
*   rfc/ext_accept_ra    = 0 (no external RFC acceptance)
*   auth/rfc_authority_check = 1 (always check RFC auth)
*   icm/security_log     = 3 (log security events)
*   snc/accept_insecure_rfc = 0
*   snc/data_protection  = 3
*
* Use RSPARAM to read and compare with SAP Security Baseline
* Report: RS_PARAMETER_CHECK

* CVA Custom code security scan
* Run from ATC (/nATC -> enter objects -> select check variant)
* Results: categorized as Error / Warning / Info
* Critical findings (Errors) must be resolved before transport

* Security note check (use /nSSA or SAP Note Assistant)
* /nSNOTE -> SAP Note Assistant
* Download notes from SAP Support Portal
* Apply to system, verify with SPAM/SAINT
* Maintain patch history in Z table for audit: Z_SAP_PATCH_HIST

* Pen test checklist entry: verify RFC function module authorization
* BAD: RFC-enabled function modules without explicit authorization
* GOOD:
FUNCTION z_my_secure_rfc.
*"----------------------------------------------------------------------
""*"RFC Enablement
" Must check authorization before processing
*"----------------------------------------------------------------------
  AUTHORITY-CHECK OBJECT 'Z_RFC_AUTH'
    ID 'ACTVT' FIELD '16'
    ID 'ZFUNC' FIELD 'Z_MY_SECURE_RFC'.

  IF sy-subrc <> 0.
    RAISE EXCEPTION TYPE cx_rfc_not_authorized.
  ENDIF.

  " Proceed with business logic
ENDFUNCTION.

* Staff+ pattern: RFC whitelist instead of blacklist
* All new RFC modules must be registered in Z_RFC_WHITELIST table
* Runtime: check if FM is in whitelist for calling system
* If not in whitelist, reject with authorization error
* This prevents unauthorized RFC execution even if auth check fails

sections.codeBlock:
" Critical profile parameters for security hardening:
" gw/reg_info          = /etc/sap/reg_info (ACL, not empty)
" gw/acl_mode          = 1 (deny anonymous RFC registration)
" login/min_password_lng = 8
" auth/rfc_authority_check = 1
" snc/accept_insecure_rfc = 0
" snc/data_protection  = 3
"
" Use RSPARAM / RS_PARAMETER_CHECK to audit
"
" RFC whitelist pattern:
FUNCTION z_my_secure_rfc.
  AUTHORITY-CHECK OBJECT 'Z_RFC_AUTH'
    ID 'ACTVT' FIELD '16'
    ID 'ZFUNC' FIELD 'Z_MY_SECURE_RFC'.
  IF sy-subrc <> 0.
    RAISE EXCEPTION TYPE cx_rfc_not_authorized.
  ENDIF.
  " Proceed with business logic
ENDFUNCTION.
"
" Monthly CVA scan via /nATC
" SAP Security Patch Day: 2nd Tuesday each month

quantifiedTradeoff.title: Reactive vs Proactive Penetration Testing Investment

variables.name: SAP system criticality
variables.source: ERP system with SOX, GDPR, PCI DSS scope

variables.name: Annual pen test cost (external)
variables.source: SAP-specific pen test: 4-week engagement, 2 senior consultants

variables.name: Average breach cost (SAP)
variables.source: IBM Cost of Data Breach 2024 — SAP systems in regulated industries

cp.monthlyPenalty: Annual proactive penetration test: $150K/year. Finds ~15 critical vulnerabilities per test. Reduces breach probability by 70%. Cost includes remediation + retesting. Net annual risk cost: $150K + ($5M × 3% residual risk) = $300K.

ap.monthlyPenalty: Reactive only (incident response + forensic investigation after breach). No upfront cost. Breach probability at industry average: 12% per year for SAP systems. Expected annual cost: 12% × $5M + $200K compliance fines = $800K.

calculation.breakeven: At breach probability above 6% per year, proactive pen testing is cheaper. For SAP systems with financial + PII data, breach probability is 10-15%, making proactive testing 2.6x cheaper on expected value. The breakeven flips for low-risk systems (no PII, no financial data, no regulatory scope).

details: Staff+ Insight: Security is the single highest-leverage concern for Staff+ SAP engineers. FAANG interviewers (especially at Google, Meta, and AWS) consistently probe security architecture — Zero Trust, identity federation, least privilege, and incident response are universal patterns. SAP security knowledge combined with cloud security principles (IAM policies, KMS, network segmentation) is rare and highly valued. Framing SAP security as an implementation of Zero Trust and ABAC shows architectural depth beyond ABAP syntax.
